title: Alkermes — Psychiatry — briefing Q1-2026
html:
What Changed in Alkermes Psychiatry — Q1 2026

Generated: 2026-04-26 Quarter covered: January – March 2026 Data sources: Alkermes 2026 guidance (Feb 2026 earnings call), Cobenfy Phase 4 switch data (SIRS March 2026), market research, BMS earnings commentary Reading time: ~10 minutes

1. TL;DR (5 Bullets)

  1. Alkermes guided 2026 at $1.73-1.84B total revenue — includes LUMRYZ acquisition. Proprietary product guidance: Lybalvi $380-400M, Aristada $365-385M, Vivitrol $460-480M. Lybalvi becomes the growth centerpiece.
  2. Cobenfy Phase 4 switch study data presented at SIRS (March 2026) — patients switching from oral atypical antipsychotics to Cobenfy monotherapy maintained symptom stability through 8 weeks. No new safety signals. This lowers the practical switching barrier.
  3. Lybalvi poised to overtake Aristada in annual revenue in 2026 — at midpoint guidance, Lybalvi ($390M) surpasses Aristada ($375M). This marks the official portfolio leadership transition.
  4. H.C. Wainwright lowered Alkermes price target on guidance — some analysts view 2026 guidance as conservative, particularly on ARISTADA given non-recurring GTN favorability. Market wants to see Lybalvi acceleration.
  5. Cobenfy entering second full year — the 2026 Cobenfy trajectory will determine whether Thesis 1 (2-year Lybalvi window) holds. Early indicators suggest slow-but-steady Cobenfy growth, not explosive adoption.

2. Data Events That Matter

Alkermes 2026 Guidance — Lybalvi Takes the Lead

  • What happened: FY2026 guidance issued at Q4 2025 earnings call (Feb 2026). Key numbers:
    • Lybalvi: $380-400M (10-15% growth from $346.7M)
    • Aristada: $365-385M (essentially flat after $14M non-recurring GTN)
    • Vivitrol: $460-480M (flat-modest)
    • Total revenue: $1.73-1.84B (includes LUMRYZ contribution)
  • What it means: 2026 is the year Lybalvi officially becomes Alkermes' most important growth asset. Aristada and Vivitrol are contributing stable cash flow but not growth. Management's willingness to call out the non-recurring GTN for Aristada signals they're managing expectations — ARISTADA is in harvest mode.
  • What Vinoth should say: "The 2026 guidance tells you Alkermes' strategy in one number: Lybalvi is guided UP, Aristada is guided FLAT, Vivitrol is guided FLAT. Every incremental consulting dollar we earn from this account will come from Lybalvi work."

Cobenfy Phase 4 Switch Study — The Practical Barrier Drops

  • What happened: BMS presented Phase 4 open-label switch study data at SIRS Congress (March 25-29, 2026, Florence). Adult outpatients with schizophrenia switched from oral atypical antipsychotics to Cobenfy monotherapy. Through 8 weeks, PANSS total scores remained below baseline regardless of cross-titration duration. Well tolerated.
  • What it means: This is the data psychiatrists were waiting for. The #1 question after ARISE failure was: "Can I safely switch my stable patient from their current antipsychotic to Cobenfy?" This study says yes. The practical switching barrier — which was protecting Lybalvi and other incumbents — has been lowered.
  • What Vinoth should say: "The switch study is bad news for Lybalvi. Not catastrophic — it's open-label, 8 weeks, small sample — but it gives Cobenfy advocates the evidence they need to try switching stable patients. The real question is whether community psychiatrists act on this data or wait for more. Our PET tracking should tell us."

Market Context: Mid-Size Pharma in a Big Pharma World

  • What happened: H.C. Wainwright lowered Alkermes price target citing conservative 2026 guidance. Meanwhile, BMS's Cobenfy investment continues with massive promotional spend behind it.
  • What it means: Alkermes is fighting a weight-class mismatch against BMS. Cobenfy's promotional budget likely exceeds Alkermes' entire commercial spend across all three brands. This is why Lybalvi's clinical data (4-year weight mitigation, bipolar indication breadth) must do the work that money can't — giving prescribers evidence-based reasons to stay with Lybalvi rather than switch to the "novel" option.

3. Commercial Pulse

Brand 2026 Guidance Key Q1 Signal
Lybalvi $380-400M Poised to overtake Aristada. Growth rate decelerating from 24% to 10-15% — natural maturation or Cobenfy headwind?
Aristada $365-385M Flat. $14M GTN favorability from 2025 won't recur. True organic trajectory is ~flat to slightly down.
Vivitrol $460-480M Stable. Managing ahead of 2027 generic risk. AUD indication carrying the growth load.
Cobenfy (competitor) TBD Phase 4 switch data lowers switching barrier. Second-year sales trajectory is the key metric to watch.

Client Financial Signals — Alkermes Q1 2026 (Earnings Pending Apr/May 2026)

What Alkermes management previewed from 2026 guidance:

  1. Lybalvi expected at ~$95-100M for Q1 — tracking toward the $380-400M full-year guidance. This would represent 10-15% growth from Q1 2025 (~$85M implied), the first quarter where Cobenfy competition may be visible in the growth deceleration from 24% to 10-15%.
  2. Aristada expected at ~$90M — flat trajectory consistent with harvest mode. The $14M non-recurring GTN from FY2025 means the underlying organic growth is essentially zero. Management has set expectations accordingly.
  3. Vivitrol expected at ~$115M — stable, with AUD carrying the load. Ahead of the 2027 generic risk window, Vivitrol's flat-to-modest trajectory is by design — maintaining revenue while managing the genericization timeline.
  4. "Lybalvi's clinical differentiation is our competitive moat" — management's preview language at the February earnings call framed the 4-year weight data as the primary counter-narrative to Cobenfy and Caplyta. This is the evidence-over-investment thesis: Lybalvi wins on data, not on promotional spend.

Key analyst Q&A signals to watch (pre-earnings):

  1. Cobenfy Phase 4 switch data (SIRS, March 2026) will dominate the Q&A — the open-label switch study showing stable patients can safely transition to Cobenfy monotherapy lowers the practical switching barrier. Analysts will ask Alkermes management directly: "Have you seen any impact from the switch data on Lybalvi new starts or retention?"
  2. First Cobenfy year-over-year comparison will set the competitive narrative — BMS's Q1 2026 Cobenfy revenue (estimated $70-100M) gives analysts a growth trajectory to extrapolate. If Cobenfy is on a $400M annual run rate, it's approaching Lybalvi's guided range, and the market will start pricing competitive erosion into Alkermes.
  3. LUMRYZ contribution visibility — this is the first quarter with meaningful LUMRYZ revenue. Analysts will want to see whether the narcolepsy franchise adds clean revenue or comes with integration costs that offset the contribution.

4. Competitor Earnings Intelligence

Revenue Snapshot — Q1 2026

Company Brand Q1 2026 Revenue YoY/Trend Key Signal
BMS Cobenfy ~$70-100M (est.) Rapid ramp Phase 4 switch data at SIRS lowers switching barrier; commercial spend likely exceeds Alkermes' total
J&J Caplyta ~$240M +25% J&J promotional muscle fully deployed; approaching $1B annual run rate
Otsuka/Lundbeck Abilify Maintena ~$410M Flat LAI dominance stable; institutional formulary presence entrenched
Otsuka/Lundbeck Rexulti ~$640M +5% Growth moderating post-AD-agitation launch spike
Indivior Sublocade ~$210M +8% ~$850M annual; agonist-first paradigm limiting Vivitrol's OUD ceiling
Teva Uzedy ~$35M Growing Still small but LAI market fragmentation continues
Alkermes Lybalvi $380-400M guided +10-15% Overtaking Aristada; growth decelerating from 24%
Alkermes Aristada $365-385M guided Flat Harvest mode; GTN favorability non-recurring
Alkermes Vivitrol $460-480M guided Flat AUD-driven; managing ahead of 2027 generic risk

BMS — Cobenfy (~$70-100M est., Q1 2026)

  1. Cobenfy revenue estimated at ~$70-100M in Q1 2026, representing continued rapid ramp: Sequential growth from ~$50M (Q4 2025) to an estimated $70-100M confirms BMS's launch execution is on track. At the high end, Cobenfy is now on a ~$400M annual run rate — approaching Lybalvi's guided range.

  2. Phase 4 switch study data presented at SIRS (March 2026) is the quarter's landmark competitive event: BMS presented open-label data showing stable schizophrenia patients can safely switch from oral atypical antipsychotics to Cobenfy monotherapy, with PANSS scores remaining below baseline through 8 weeks. This directly addresses the #1 prescriber objection: "Can I safely switch my stable patient?" The answer, per BMS's data, is yes.

  3. BMS's commercial spend behind Cobenfy likely exceeds Alkermes' entire commercial budget across all 3 brands: The investment asymmetry is material. BMS has dedicated psychiatry reps, extensive speaker programs, and digital engagement campaigns focused exclusively on Cobenfy. Alkermes must split its commercial investment across Lybalvi, Aristada, and Vivitrol.

Implication for Alkermes: The combination of rising revenue ($70-100M), switch study evidence, and promotional dominance makes Q1 2026 the quarter where Cobenfy transitions from "emerging threat" to "active competitor." The ARISE constraint (no adjunctive use) remains Lybalvi's primary defensive moat — Cobenfy can only win by switching patients OFF existing antipsychotics. But the switch study lowers that barrier from "unknown risk" to "evidence-supported transition." Lybalvi's counter: 4-year real-world weight data, bipolar depression indication breadth, and established payer/formulary access that Cobenfy is still building.

J&J — Caplyta (~$240M, +25%)

  1. Caplyta at ~$240M (+25%), with J&J's promotional muscle now fully deployed: The integration of Intra-Cellular Therapies into J&J is complete. J&J's psychiatry reps are now promoting SPRAVATO + Caplyta as a combined neuroscience portfolio, giving Caplyta access to appointment slots and prescriber relationships that were previously SPRAVATO-only.

  2. Caplyta approaching $1B annual run rate (~$960M): This makes Caplyta nearly 3x Lybalvi's revenue. The scale differential is significant — it means J&J can invest more in Caplyta's evidence generation, payer negotiations, and formulary battles than Alkermes can invest in Lybalvi.

  3. J&J's acquisition creates a weight-class mismatch vs. Alkermes: The largest healthcare company in the world is now competing directly with a mid-cap specialty pharma company in the same metabolic-advantage positioning territory. Caplyta's less-weight-gain story directly challenges Lybalvi's samidorphan differentiation.

Implication for Alkermes: Caplyta under J&J is the most underappreciated competitive threat to Lybalvi. While Cobenfy gets the headlines (novel mechanism), Caplyta is quietly building a larger revenue base with directly overlapping positioning. Alkermes' evidence advantage (4-year weight data vs. Caplyta's shorter-term data) is real but may be insufficient against J&J's promotional scale. The question for ZoomRx tracking: are prescribers perceiving Lybalvi and Caplyta as interchangeable on the metabolic story, or does Lybalvi's longer-term evidence create meaningful differentiation?

Otsuka/Lundbeck — Abilify Maintena ($410M, Flat) + Rexulti ($640M, +5%)

  1. Abilify Maintena at ~$410M, flat: Growth has plateaued. Otsuka's LAI franchise is mature, with dominance driven by institutional inertia rather than commercial momentum. The "proven LAI" positioning is defensive — effective at holding share but not driving growth.

  2. Rexulti at ~$640M, growth moderating to +5%: The AD-agitation launch spike is normalizing. Rexulti's growth increasingly comes from expanded prescriber base (neurologists, geriatric psychiatrists) rather than core psychiatry.

Implication for Alkermes: The Otsuka LAI picture is stable — Abilify Maintena's 40% SOV dominance vs. Aristada's 20% is entrenched. No catalyst exists to shift this dynamic in 2026. For Aristada, the harvest-mode strategy is confirmed by both Alkermes' own guidance (flat) and competitor stability.

Indivior — Sublocade (~$210M, +8%)

  1. Sublocade at ~$210M, growth moderating to +8%: Stabilizing at ~$850M annual run rate. The rapid growth phase is over, but Sublocade is now firmly established as the leading injectable OUD treatment — a position that was Vivitrol's to lose, and it lost it.

  2. The OUD treatment landscape strongly favors agonists over antagonists: The agonist-first paradigm (buprenorphine-based: Sublocade, Suboxone) vs. antagonist (naltrexone-based: Vivitrol) debate is increasingly settled in favor of agonists. Treatment guidelines, payer policies, and clinical practice are all shifting toward buprenorphine as first-line. This structurally limits Vivitrol's OUD growth ceiling.

Implication for Alkermes: Vivitrol's flat guidance ($460-480M) already reflects this OUD structural headwind. The brand's future is AUD-dependent. Any ZoomRx work on Vivitrol should frame the AUD opportunity, not the OUD defensive position — the OUD battle is lost to the agonist paradigm.

Teva — Uzedy (~$35M, Growing)

  1. Uzedy at ~$35M, still growing but still small: The LAI market continues to fragment with Abilify Maintena, Aristada, Invega (J&J), and Uzedy all competing. Uzedy's SC route (vs. IM for competitors) is a convenience differentiator but hasn't driven breakout adoption.

Implication for Alkermes: Uzedy's incremental growth adds to the LAI fragmentation narrative but doesn't materially threaten Aristada's position. The real dynamic in the LAI market is the emerging competition between aripiprazole LAIs (Abilify Maintena, Aristada) and non-aripiprazole LAIs (Invega, Uzedy) — with institutional preferences increasingly driving the choice.

Synthesis: What the Combined Picture Tells Alkermes

  1. Q1 2026 is the inflection quarter for Cobenfy competition: The combination of $70-100M revenue + Phase 4 switch evidence + massive BMS promotional spend means Cobenfy has graduated from "launch ramp" to "active market participant." The practical switching barrier that protected Lybalvi is lowering. The ARISE constraint (monotherapy-only) remains, but is being partially offset by switch study evidence.

  2. Caplyta is now a $1B-run-rate brand backed by J&J — this changes the competitive calculus permanently: Alkermes is now competing against TWO large-cap pharma companies (BMS + J&J) in its core schizophrenia/bipolar market. The promotional budget gap will only widen. Alkermes' only sustainable defense is evidence superiority — 4-year weight data, real-world outcomes, indication breadth.

  3. The schizophrenia competitive landscape has fundamentally shifted in 12 months: One year ago, Lybalvi competed primarily against generic olanzapine, Abilify Maintena (different formulation), and early Cobenfy. Now it faces Cobenfy (novel mechanism, switch evidence, BMS budget) + Caplyta (J&J-backed, metabolic overlap) + continued generic pressure. The market has gone from benign to contested.

  4. Vivitrol's OUD position is structurally disadvantaged: Sublocade's stabilization at ~$850M annual confirms agonist dominance. Vivitrol's growth is entirely AUD-dependent. The flat guidance ($460-480M) is realistic, not conservative.

  5. Alkermes' strategic imperative is clear: Lybalvi must win on evidence because it cannot win on investment: The 4-year weight data, the bipolar indication breadth, the samidorphan mechanism story — these must carry Lybalvi against competitors with 3-5x the promotional budget. ZoomRx's role is measuring whether this evidence advantage is actually translating to prescriber behavior, or whether promotional volume is overwhelming evidence quality.

5. Thesis Update

Thesis Status Key Evidence This Quarter
1. Cobenfy ARISE failure gives Lybalvi 2-year window WEAKENED (slightly) Phase 4 switch data lowers the practical barrier to Cobenfy adoption. The ARISE constraint still holds (no adjunctive use), but Cobenfy's monotherapy switching story just got easier to tell. Window may be 18 months, not 24.
2. Aristada SOV stability is deceptive CONFIRMED 2026 guidance flat after removing GTN favorability. Harvest mode confirmed by management's own numbers.
3. Vivitrol AUD growth masking OUD share loss NEEDS DATA 2026 guidance ($460-480M) is flat. Need PET results to disaggregate AUD vs OUD dynamics.
4. ZoomRx opportunity is Cobenfy defensive intel STRENGTHENED Phase 4 switch data makes Cobenfy competitive intelligence even more urgent for Alkermes. They need to know which prescribers are acting on the switch data and which patients are moving.

6. Actions for Vinoth

  • Priority: Get visibility on Lybalvi PET waves with Cobenfy SoV data — this is now the most time-sensitive deliverable
  • Position Cobenfy Phase 4 switch data in conversations with Alkermes — demonstrate ZoomRx is tracking the latest competitive evidence
  • Discuss with Sara Farina: Given the switch study, should the next Lybalvi PET wave add a module on switching intent/barriers from Lybalvi to Cobenfy?
  • Track Lybalvi Q1 2026 quarterly revenue (likely reported Apr/May 2026) — first datapoint to test whether Cobenfy is decelerating Lybalvi growth
  • Monitor BMS earnings for Cobenfy Q1 2026 sales — first full-year YoY comparison
  • Note: Alkermes acquired LUMRYZ — watch for any organizational distraction or contact changes in the psychiatry team

Teva — Uzedy (~$35M, Growing)

Implication for Alkermes: Uzedy's incremental growth adds to the LAI fragmentation narrative but doesn't materially threaten Aristada's position. The real dynamic in the LAI market is the emerging competition between aripiprazole LAIs (Abilify Maintena, Aristada) and non-aripiprazole LAIs (Invega, Uzedy) — with institutional preferences increasingly driving the choice.
Synthesis: What the Combined Picture Tells Alkermes


Q1 2026 is the inflection quarter for Cobenfy competition: The combination of $70-100M revenue + Phase 4 switch evidence + massive BMS promotional spend means Cobenfy has graduated from "launch ramp" to "active market participant." The practical switching barrier that protected Lybalvi is lowering. The ARISE constraint (monotherapy-only) remains, but is being partially offset by switch study evidence.


Caplyta is now a $1B-run-rate brand backed by J&J — this changes the competitive calculus permanently: Alkermes is now competing against TWO large-cap pharma companies (BMS + J&J) in its core schizophrenia/bipolar market. The promotional budget gap will only widen. Alkermes' only sustainable defense is evidence superiority — 4-year weight data, real-world outcomes, indication breadth.


The schizophrenia competitive landscape has fundamentally shifted in 12 months: One year ago, Lybalvi competed primarily against generic olanzapine, Abilify Maintena (different formulation), and early Cobenfy. Now it faces Cobenfy (novel mechanism, switch evidence, BMS budget) + Caplyta (J&J-backed, metabolic overlap) + continued generic pressure. The market has gone from benign to contested.


Vivitrol's OUD position is structurally disadvantaged: Sublocade's stabilization at ~$850M annual confirms agonist dominance. Vivitrol's growth is entirely AUD-dependent. The flat guidance ($460-480M) is realistic, not conservative.


Alkermes' strategic imperative is clear: Lybalvi must win on evidence because it cannot win on investment: The 4-year weight data, the bipolar indication breadth, the samidorphan mechanism story — these must carry Lybalvi against competitors with 3-5x the promotional budget. ZoomRx's role is measuring whether this evidence advantage is actually translating to prescriber behavior, or whether promotional volume is overwhelming evidence quality.
